title: Joojeh kebab
subtitle: Persian saffron and lemon chicken
excerpt: Joojeh kebab is the everyday Persian chicken kebab: boneless thighs marinated in saffron water, lemon, and onion, then cooked over high heat until charred at the edges and juicy through the middle. Saffron does the work — a pinch gives the chicken a golden hue and a faintly floral note that no other spice replicates.
sourceNotes:
• Margaret Shaida, *The Legendary Cuisine of Persia* (1992). Foundation reference for the saffron-water technique and the marinade proportions.
• Najmieh Batmanglij, *Food of Life* (2011). Cross-reference for the joojeh kebab canon and the cooking method over charcoal or on a griddle.

recipe.batchNotes: The marinade can be made in a larger batch and frozen with the raw chicken. Defrost overnight in the fridge and cook from chilled.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Marinate the chicken up to 24 hours ahead in the fridge. The longer it sits in the saffron marinade, the more flavour it takes on.

glossaryTerms.definition: Joojeh means young chicken or chick in Persian. In the context of joojeh kebab, it refers to chicken marinated in saffron, lemon, and onion, then cooked quickly over high heat — a staple of Persian restaurants and home kitchens alike.

content.text: The bloom is where
joojeh

kebab starts: a pinch of saffron dissolved in two tablespoons of warm water until the liquid turns the colour of strong tea. That saffron water goes into the marinade alongside lemon juice and grated onion, and the chicken sits in it for at least two hours — four if you have the time. By the time it hits a hot griddle, the colour is already in the meat.

Boneless chicken thighs cook faster and more forgivingly than breasts, and they hold the marinade better. The skin can go on or off depending on preference; skinless thighs are easier to eat off skewers, and the marinade penetrates more directly. On a griddle or cast-iron pan, the edges will char slightly at around the 5-minute mark on each side — that char is part of the point.
Serve with steamed basmati rice, charred flatbread, and a small bowl of fresh herbs — radishes, spring onions, and flat-leaf parsley are the traditional accompaniment.
What you need

A heavy ridged griddle pan gives the best char marks and can be left on a high heat for the minutes needed. A cast-iron skillet works equally well. If cooking outdoors over charcoal, thread the pieces onto flat metal skewers and cook closer to the flame.
Method
Bloom the saffron

Crumble {{saffron}} of saffron into a small bowl. Pour over 2 tablespoons of warm water (not boiling) and leave for 5 minutes. The water should turn a deep amber. This blooming step extracts the colour and flavour more fully than adding the strands directly to the marinade.
Make the marinade and coat the chicken

In a large bowl, combine the saffron water with the juice of {{lemon}} lemons, {{onion}} grated onion (squeezed), {{olive-oil}} of olive oil, {{black-pepper}} of black pepper, and a generous pinch of salt. Add {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thighs and turn to coat thoroughly. Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours, up to 24.
Cook the chicken

Bring the chicken to room temperature 30 minutes before cooking. Heat a griddle pan or heavy frying pan over the highest heat until smoking. Remove excess marinade from each piece but don't dry it completely — a thin coating stays on. Cook in batches if needed; don't crowd the pan. Cook for 5 to 6 minutes on the first side without moving, until char marks appear and the edges look set. Turn and cook for another 4 to 5 minutes until cooked through.
Rest and serve
Transfer to a warm plate and rest for 5 minutes. Serve with lemon wedges alongside.

items.symptom: Chicken is pale rather than golden
items.cause: Not enough saffron was bloomed, or the pan was not hot enough.
items.fix: Make sure the saffron is properly bloomed (deep amber water) and the pan is at full heat before the chicken goes in. The golden colour comes from the saffron — the char marks from the heat.

items.cause: Breast pieces were used, or the pan was too cool, extending the cooking time.
items.fix: Use boneless thighs, which hold moisture much better than breasts. Check the internal temperature: 74°C at the thickest point means done.

items.cause: The griddle was not hot enough, or too much wet marinade was left on the chicken.
items.fix: Let the griddle heat until smoking before adding the chicken. Shake off excess marinade — a thin coat is right. Once the chicken releases naturally, it is ready to turn.

items.cause: Pan was not fully preheated, or the chicken was moved too early.
items.fix: A ridged griddle pan needs 2 to 3 minutes over full heat before it can sear properly. Once the chicken is down, don't touch it for at least 5 minutes.

content.text: Variations

The same marinade works with bone-in chicken thighs and drumsticks cooked on a barbecue; allow 25 to 30 minutes over medium coals. A pinch of cinnamon and a teaspoon of grated ginger added to the marinade gives a warmer version. Whole spatchcocked chicken can be marinated and roasted at 220°C (fan 200°C) for 40 minutes.
Where this dish lives

Joojeh kebab is one of the foundational dishes of Persian restaurant cooking, appearing on kebabi menus alongside koobideh (minced lamb) and barg (lamb fillet). In home kitchens it is more casual: chicken thighs marinated and cooked on a regular hob pan rather than over an open fire. The saffron, though expensive by weight, goes far — a single good pinch colours and flavours a four-person meal. It is the element that makes an otherwise simple dish distinctly Persian.
